reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. strengths: All four database guardrail types are fully covered with working code examples, decision-support prose (When to enable / When to skip / Tradeoff), and an error message illustration per guardrail â€” the target audience gets everything they need to act without digging further.

The combined 'all four guardrails at once' example near the end is the most practical section on the page: it shows what a production-ready database config looks like and explains why each property is there.
Clear preventive-vs-reactive distinction (guardrails vs. alarms) is established at the top and reinforced in the FAQ â€” this is a confusion point for first-time users and the page handles it well.
Tables are used exactly where they add value: the guardrail summary table at the top, the accessibility-mode compliance table, and the engine category table all let a scanning reader orient quickly.

FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service mechanics, comparison/choosing) and includes a practical 'Should I enable all four?' answer that the audience will actually search for.
problems: The specific error message formats shown in each section (e.g., 'Database "myDb" uses engine "mysql" which is not allowed. If the real messages differ, readers will be confused when they hit a violation.

The engine type table (postgres, mysql, aurora-postgresql, aurora-mysql-serverless-v2, etc.) is not grounded in the provided guardrails.d.ts source; the source only defines allowedEngines as string[]. These names come from relational-database types which were not provided. The table is likely accurate but presents itself with full authority the source doesn't give it.

The accessibility-mode compliance table (marking 'internet' and 'whitelisted-ips-only' as non-compliant, 'vpc' and 'scoping-workloads-in-vpc' as compliant) is not sourced from guardrails.d.ts, which only defines enabled?: boolean. The specific modes are from the relational-database resource type, not provided as a source here.
The claim that guardrails are enforced during deploy, codebuild:deploy, preview-changes, and delete commands is specific operational behavior not grounded in the provided source file.

The Aurora Serverless exemption from instance restriction ('Aurora Serverless v1 and v2 engines have no fixed instance sizes â€” they scale capacity automatically. optionalImprovements: Mark error message examples as illustrative: add a short qualifier like '(error message is illustrative â€” exact wording may differ)' so readers aren't surprised when the real message looks different.

Qualify the engine type table with a note that these names map to the engine.type property on RelationalDatabase and link to the relational database page for the authoritative list. This avoids the page becoming a maintenance burden if engines are added/removed.

Qualify the accessibility-mode compliance table with a link to the relational database accessibility docs rather than asserting it inline, since the authority for those mode names sits on the relational database page.

Add a sentence in the Aurora Serverless exemption explaining the reasoning explicitly: 'Aurora Serverless has no configurable instance size property in the Stacktape config, so the guardrail has nothing to check' â€” this grounds the claim in observable config behavior rather than an asserted rule.

The 'How database guardrails work' section repeats content from the intro paragraph (preventive, fails before CloudFormation, named resource error). reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria â€” penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense â€” its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' â€” never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose â€” tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine â€” quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context â€” every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page â€” not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth â€” AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic â€” examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage â€” that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Overview table linking all four guardrail types with type identifiers and key properties gives readers an instant orientation.
Preventive vs reactive distinction is surfaced early via an Info callout and stays consistent throughout the page â€” this is a real conceptual trap for new users and the page handles it well.
Per-guardrail 'when to enable / when to skip / tradeoff' structure is consistent, opinionated, and concise. Readers can quickly decide whether a guardrail applies to them.
Allowlist vs blocklist framing for engine restriction vs instance restriction is genuinely useful â€” it names a real difference that affects how teams maintain the policy over time.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service mechanics, choosing/comparison) with solid 2-4 sentence answers. Questions are realistic and not padded.
problems: Critical content gap: the page never shows how to configure guardrails in defineConfig. The reader finishes the page knowing what each guardrail does but having no idea how to write one in stacktape.ts. This is the primary thing the target audience needs.

The only code example ('Combining database guardrails') shows a relational database resource that would comply with guardrails â€” not the guardrail definitions themselves. The section title and framing suggest it will show combined guardrails, but it delivers a database config instead. This is backwards and will confuse a first-time reader who came looking for how to set up guardrails.

The RelationalDatabase engine in the code example uses a plain-object format `{ type: 'aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2', properties: {...} }` rather than the class-based packaging pattern used elsewhere in Stacktape configs (e.g., `new AuroraServerlessV2EnginePostgresql({...})`). The provided source files (guardrails.d.ts only) do not include RelationalDatabase types, so this cannot be verified â€” but if the format is wrong, the example will not type-check.
No ApiReference component for any of the four guardrail type definitions, leaving readers with no structured property reference to consult after reading the prose.
mandatoryFixes: Add a <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{label: 'TypeScript', ...}]} /> example showing how to actually define database guardrails in defineConfig â€” i.e., a guardrails section or guardrail resource in the Stacktape config that uses the type identifiers ('database-engine-restriction', 'database-instance-restriction', etc.). This is the first thing the target audience needs and it is entirely absent from the page.

Restructure the 'Combining database guardrails' section so it leads with the guardrail configuration (the guardrail definitions in stacktape.ts), not with a relational database that happens to comply. The current example answers 'what does a compliant database look like' rather than 'how do I configure these guardrails'. Show the guardrail definitions first; the compliant database resource can follow as supporting context.
optionalImprovements: Move a minimal single-guardrail configuration example (e.g., just engine restriction) to near the top of the page â€” before the per-guardrail detail sections â€” so readers can see the syntax immediately and orient themselves before reading the explanations.

Add an <ApiReference definitionName="..." /> for the guardrail union or individual guardrail props types so readers have a structured property reference. Even a governance page benefits from this when the underlying types are well-defined.

Verify the RelationalDatabase engine configuration format in the code example against the actual relational-database type definitions. If the correct form is class-based (e.g., new AuroraServerlessV2EnginePostgresql({...})), update the example to match â€” mismatched syntax in code examples erodes reader trust.

The intro paragraph mentions relational databases as the target of database guardrails. Clarify whether require-vpc-databases and require-deletion-protection apply only to RelationalDatabase resources or to other database resource types (Redis, DynamoDB, etc.) as well â€” first-time readers will not know the scope without this.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices â€” only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Correctly identifies all four database-specific guardrail types from the source and describes each one accurately against the type definitions.
Clearly distinguishes preventive guardrails from reactive alarms in the opening Info callout â€” this is a common source of confusion and the page addresses it immediately.
Allowlist vs blocklist distinction for engine restriction vs instance restriction is explained well and helps the reader reason about edge cases.
Per-guardrail 'When to enable / When to skip / Tradeoff' structure is consistent and decision-supportive.
FAQ section is broad and mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-mechanics, and comparison questions â€” good AEO coverage.
Intro table summarizing all four guardrails with type identifiers and key properties is a strong scannability win.
problems: CRITICAL: The page never shows how to define database guardrails in a Stacktape config. The single code example is a compliant database config, not a guardrail definition. An engineer reading this page learns what each guardrail enforces but cannot implement any of them â€” there is no example of the guardrail array entry syntax (type + properties) in TypeScript. This is the most important practical gap on the page.

Unsourced scope claim: The FAQ answer for 'Can I apply different database guardrails to different stages?' states 'Database guardrails apply as organization-level policies.' The source file (guardrails.d.ts) says nothing about org-level scope. This is an invented constraint that could mislead engineers about how guardrails are scoped.

The 'Combining database guardrails' section's code example shows a compliant RelationalDatabase + LambdaFunction config but no guardrail definitions. A reader building a complete mental model cannot tell from this page what the guardrail configuration looks like in the config that enforces those rules.

Minor: The engine example uses 'aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2' but the guardrail source's allowedEngines JSDoc example gives only 'postgres' and 'aurora-postgresql'. The page does not clarify whether 'aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2' is a distinct engine type string the guardrail would check, which creates potential confusion about whether the compliant example would actually pass the given allowlist.
mandatoryFixes: Add at least one CodeBlock showing the guardrail definition configuration syntax â€” the actual guardrail array entries with type and properties fields in defineConfig. Without this, the page fails its primary job: enabling an engineer to configure database guardrails. Minimally, show one complete guardrail definition (e.g., database-engine-restriction with allowedEngines) and note that others follow the same pattern. This must be a <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{label: 'TypeScript', ...}]} /> block, not prose.

Remove or source the 'Database guardrails apply as organization-level policies' claim in the FAQ. The source file is silent on scope. Either ground the claim in a referenced source, or replace the sentence with a scoped, cautious statement that directs the reader to the guardrails overview page for scope details.
optionalImprovements: For each per-guardrail H2 section (engine restriction, instance restriction, etc.), consider adding a small inline TypeScript snippet showing the guardrail definition for that specific type, instead of reserving all examples for a single combined section at the bottom. This pattern helps engineers who are skimming to configure a single guardrail without reading the full page.

Clarify the relationship between 'aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2' as used in the code example and the allowedEngines value 'aurora-postgresql' from the source JSDoc. If they are different strings, the compliant config example actually illustrates an engine restriction bypass â€” a misleading setup for readers trying to reason about the engine guardrail.

Add a note about what happens to existing non-compliant deployments when a guardrail is introduced (e.g., whether the guardrail applies only to new deployments or also blocks updates to stacks that were already deployed with non-compliant databases). The source is silent here, so phrase it cautiously if adding.

The page links to '/guardrails/deployment', '/guardrails/security-and-data-protection', and '/guardrails/resource-limits'. Confirm these routes exist in the docs nav; if the route for 'resource limit guardrails' is different, update the link.

In the instance restriction Warning callout, consider giving a concrete example of the blocklist gap: if you only block 'db.r5.4xlarge' but the same-generation 'db.r5.8xlarge' and 'db.r5.16xlarge' remain available, the cost ceiling is not meaningfully enforced. This sharpens the tradeoff.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria â€” penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense â€” its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' â€” never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose â€” tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine â€” quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context â€” every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page â€” not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth â€” AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic â€” examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes preventive deployment policy from runtime monitoring.
The page uses compact H2 headings, a useful summary table, and a broad FAQ that mixes Stacktape-specific, comparison, and AWS-adjacent questions.
The four database guardrail identifiers and their primary properties match the provided guardrails type source.
No forbidden YAML Stacktape config, StacktapeConfig typed style, or fenced TypeScript Stacktape config appears.
problems: Several technical claims go beyond the only provided source file, especially around relational database engine variants, Aurora/RDS behavior, VPC networking requirements, bastion access, compliance frameworks, cost behavior, and AWS deletion protection mechanics.

The combined configuration example uses RelationalDatabase, LambdaFunction, packaging, engine shape, accessibility mode, connectTo, and joinDefaultVpc, none of which are grounded in the provided guardrails.d.ts source for this page.

The page says the full set of allowedEngines matches relational database engine options, but the provided source only says allowedEngines is string[] and gives examples postgres and aurora-postgresql.

The VPC-only section overstates implementation details: the source only says databases must use VPC-only accessibility with no public internet access, not that consuming workloads must be in the same VPC or that bastion hosts are the supported local access path.

The stage-level guidance in the FAQ is likely misleading: the source shows stage-restriction has allowedStages, but does not show guardrail composition or conditional application of database guardrails by stage.

The page is practical as a policy guide, but it lacks an example of the guardrail definition itself; the only code example shows a compliant database workload rather than how to configure database guardrails.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or narrow unsupported Stacktape-specific claims that are not grounded in guardrails.d.ts, especially relational database engine catalogs, VPC consumer networking, bastion host use, guardrail-by-stage composition, and deletion-protection deletion workflow.

Replace the combined compliant workload example with a source-grounded example of database guardrail definitions, or omit the code example if this page's source set does not provide a supported Stacktape config surface for guardrails.

Revise the allowedEngines wording to say only that the property is string[] and the type definition gives postgres and aurora-postgresql as examples; do not claim a full engine list unless that source is provided.

Each guardrail section follows a consistent shape: definition snippet, When to enable, When to skip, Tradeoff. The repetition is helpful, not redundant.

AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria â€” penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense â€” its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' â€” never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose â€” tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine â€” quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context â€” every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page â€” not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth â€” AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic â€” examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria â€” penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense â€” its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' â€” never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose â€” tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine â€” quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context â€” every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page â€” not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth â€” AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic â€” examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
